Far-right groups are increasingly filling this gap to spread their message,"Plus, Harlan Crow rebuffs Democrats’ questions about gifts to Clarence Thomas ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ When Disaster Strikes, Far-Right Groups See An Opportunity To Gain Trust Stewart Rhodes, the infamous leader of the anti-government Oath Keeper militia, was standing on a street in Conroe, Texas, a city about 40 miles north of Houston. The sky was clear blue, but remnants of darker days were everywhere. Residents were shoveling up splintered lumber and debris. A boy holding a broom was halfheartedly scooping lawn scraps into a garbage bag a few feet away from where Rhodes was conducting an on-camera interview.A Category 4 hurricane named Harvey had just dumped feet, not inches, of water on the state, sparking one of the most expensive disasters in United States history. The scale of the damage was so vast that the then-director of the Federal Emergency Management Agency put out a request for volunteers. “We need citizens to be involved,” he said on Aug. 28, 2017, a few days after the storm struck Texas. The Oath Keepers answered the call.Rhodes was wearing an Oath Keepers cap and T-shirt. He was there with another Oath Keeper, the organization’s southeast regional assistant coordinator, Alex Oakes. The men were interviewing Beau Sullivan, a Conroe local who had been organizing hurricane relief efforts after the storm.“Thank you gentlemen for coming out here,” Sullivan said, shaking Rhodes’ and Oakes’ hands. “You know, normally y’all gotta be a little more brass tacks, but y’all come out here with a message of love this time, and camaraderie, and I really appreciate that. That’s what’s needed now in this rebuilding effort.”The exchange, captured on video and disseminated by the Oath Keepers on AltCensored, a right-wing alternative to YouTube, neatly distills why a group mainly preoccupied with uncovering made-up evidence of government tyranny might participate in hurricane relief efforts: It wins people over. Read more Conservative billionaire Harlan Crow responded Monday night to a request from Senate judiciary committee Democrats for more details about gifts he’s given to Supreme Court Justice Clarence Thomas that have not been disclosed. Crow’s response? I’m not telling you anything. Democrats on the Senate committee, led by Chairman Dick Durbin (D-Ill.), demanded earlier this month that Crow provide them with a list of all gifts in excess of $415 that he has given to Supreme Court justices. Their letter came in response to ProPublica’s bombshell report last month that Thomas has been accepting luxury trips and gifts from Crow virtually every year for more than two decades without disclosing them. Read More Rep. James Comer (R-Ky.) on Monday inadvertently implied that House Republicans’ high-profile investigation into President Joe Biden’s family members and their finances is actually about helping Donald Trump win the presidency in 2024. Comer, who is leading the GOP’s probe as chair of the House oversight and accountability committee, appeared to say the quiet part out loud during a “Fox & Friends First” interview. Read More Donald Trump’s trial over hush money payments to porn star Stormy Daniels will begin March 25, 2024, a New York City judge ruled Tuesday. The former president, who faces more than 30 felony charges of falsifying business records, appeared virtually in Manhattan court Tuesday to hear Judge Juan Merchan’s decision on the date. Trump, who has pleaded not guilty on all counts, scowled upon learning the date, ABC News reported. The trial will kick off right in the middle of the 2024 presidential primaries, for which he’s a declared Republican candidate. Is this something that can be configured through the API monitoring dashboard, or would it require a more custom solution?

Re: server is rejecting mail from single domain,"NOW I get it, I thought that postfix was complaining because host lookup of the FQDN does not match the IP. But to be honest, it looks to me like someone has owned their server and having a bit of fun, because the server is literally announcing itself as ""Spam-Server"" in the HELO instead of the FQDN! $ telnet 64.22.240.195 25 Trying 64.22.240.195... Connected to 64.22.240.195. Escape character is '^]'. 220 Spam-Server Microsoft ESMTP MAIL Service, Version: 6.0.3790.1830 ready at Fri, 8 Feb 2008 16:55:39 -0800 Sandy Drobic wrote: > Jonathan Dill wrote: >> James D. Parra wrote: >>> I can suggest that to him, but this is a client so I can only ask. >>> From the >>> client's vantage point, our server is the only one rejecting his >>> mail, so I >>> need to tread politely. >>> >>> Concerning removing reject_non_fqdn_hostname (or >>> reject_non_fqdn_helo_hostname) is not something I want to do. If this >>> weren't a client of ours I wouldn't worry too much about it, but it >>> is and I >>> must. Is there a way could add an exception to the main.cf for this >>> domain >>> or IP address? >>> >> >> If he sent e-mail to me, my server would also reject it, then you >> would not be alone. But seriously, I wonder if setting up >> /etc/nsswitch.conf to check ""files"" first and adding an entry to >> /etc/hosts would fix it e.g. >> >> 64.22.240.195 ns.ky3.com >> >> I am not sure how postfix verifies fqdn, if it uses the OS resolver >> stack or some other method. > > It is not the client hostname that is rejected, it's the helo name > that the client itself determines to announces itself to the receiving > server. > > This is especially stupid since you have to configure the helo of your > own system. > > So you can only kick the admin of the server with the non-fqdn-helo or > whitelist the client. >",0,1

Enough With The Corporate 'Therapy Speak',"Forbes NewslettersPlus: The Fastest-Growing Jobs For Young WorkersADVERTISEMENTBECOME A MEMBER • Newsletters • MyForbesAt some point, you’ve probably heard your boss refer to your co-workers as “family.” But for workers, the practice is no longer a sign of strong company culture. It’s a red flag.Amid continued economic uncertainty, mass layoffs and a growing “perk-cession,” 67% of the 800 U.S. employees surveyed by the Harris Poll reported experiencing “therapy speak” at work—empathetic-sounding language companies use to show understanding such as “we understand these are challenging times,” “we’re like family” and “we’re all in this together.” But employees are increasingly skeptical: 71% said they see through the inauthentic communications while 69% called it hypocritical, the report released Tuesday found.“We're hitting this tipping point of, ‘I'd rather you say nothing at all than say something just to say something without the action to back it up,’” Harris Poll’s Abbey Lunney told me. The result? Disengaged employees and resentment. Meanwhile, tech’s slew of layoffs is only further busting the “we are a family” belief—because “when things go wrong, a family does not fire its kids,” senior contributor Jack Kelly wrote. Salesforce cofounder and CEO Marc Benioff’s use of “ohana” to describe company culture, for instance, received rampant backlash following the company’s January layoff of nearly 8,000 employees.“[When] the CEO email lands incorrectly—it rubs someone the wrong way or whatever it is—it impacts people’s entire relationship with work,” Lunney says. “Employers think that they’re doing a great job. They’re talking the talk, embracing modern mental health best practices, trying to make their employees feel valued. But they’re totally missing the mark.”While empathy is an essential leadership skill everyone should develop, knowing how to communicate that skill in a way people will appreciate is even more important—and can help you perform under pressure.As always, we hope you find practical insights and advice for your careers in this newsletter. I’m excited to get started bringing you news and ideas each week about the job market, careers and getting ahead—you’ll find me in your inbox on Tuesdays, and I hope you enjoy its new look. Let me know what you want to read! Oh, and don’t forget to sign up to tune in virtually to our Future of Work Summit next week. Have a great one.Emmy Lucas General Assignment ReporterFollow me on Twitter and Forbes.comWORK SMARTERPractical insights and advice from Forbes contributors to help you succeed in your job, accelerate your career and lead smarterThink layoffs may hit your company soon? Here’s what you can do in the waiting period—besides worry.More than 100 H.R. professionals share advice on how to make your LinkedIn profile stand out.Is our obsession with time actually killing productivity?You’ve heard of the STAR method. Now it’s time to use it in your next interview.Self-confidence can be hard. But there are ways to boost it to achieve your goals.Featured StoryThe number of U.S. workers testing positive for cannabis increased 10.3%. GETTY IMAGESA Record Number Of American Workers Are Using Cannabis—That Doesn’t Mean They’re High On The JobRead ArticleU.S. workers set a 25-year record in testing positive for marijuana use. Quest Diagnostics found marijuana positivity increased by 10.3% in just one year, from 2021 to 2022.Legalization critics like Kevin Sabet believe the Quest Diagnostics report is a bad omen: “I think it's worrisome that we're seeing this, especially at our workplaces and in safety sensitive positions, putting other people at risk…The issue is whether we think it's okay to be a stoned society or not.” But as any occasional toker who’s had to pass a drug test knows, that data does not mean workers are actually getting high on the job. Forbes’ Will Yakowicz explores why the report’s results are misleading.TOUCH BASEEUROPA PRESS VIA GETTY IMAGESNews from the world of workBosses crack down on AI: Apple is the latest company in a growing list that includes Samsung, Amazon and JPMorgan Chase to place restrictions on the use of artificial intelligence for employees, Forbes’ Siladitya Ray reports. The company’s concern? Staffers may leak sensitive internal data.What to know about ‘imposter syndrome’: Forbes’ Conor Murray breaks down the common psychological phenomenon that makes people doubt their successes.Musk says remote work is ‘morally wrong’: In a CNBC interview last week, billionaire Elon Musk said remote workers—the “laptop class”—are leveraging an unfair privilege and that remote work is “morally wrong.” Forbes Senior Editor Jena McGregor writes in our new Future of Work newsletter that “that argument, while acknowledging the disparate class impact of the pandemic, ignores remote work’s morally positive benefits.”For young workers, LinkedIn identifies the fastest-growing jobs and industries: As layoffs mount and economic headwinds continue, “there are bright spots,” says LinkedIn Career Expert Andrew McCaskill. Contributor Mark Perna takes a look at LinkedIn’s 2023 Guide to Kickstarting Your Career.The impact of golf-loving bosses: Researchers from two universities analyzed the executive teams of more than 4,000 CEOs over a 12-year period, finding that women are 12% less likely to be represented in the C-suite when the CEO plays golf. Contributor Corinne Post dives into the study.ADVERTISEMENTCHECKLISTAdd these books and videos to your to-do listIn Spark: 24 Concepts to Ignite, Unstick or Supercharge Your Work Life, authors Chris Mettler and Jon Yarian explore what motivates individuals, teams and organizations to succeed.Will.i.am, entrepreneur and cofounder of The Black-Eyed Peas, sits down with Forbes Assistant Managing Editor Diane Brady to talk about his innovative artist-in-residence program with Formula 1.NUMBER TO NOTE25%The percentage of workers who said they’re confident about their career path, according to Gartner.Contributor Caroline Castrillon provides five strategies to help you professionally advance your career—so you can feel happier and more fulfilled at work.QUIZFollowing in the footsteps of similar legislation passed by California and New York, which state recently moved to ban warehouses from firing workers over undisclosed quotas? It’s the latest state to enact protections geared toward Amazon fulfillment workers.A.WyomingB.MinnesotaC.WashingtonD.Illinois Check if you got it right here.ACROSS THE NEWSROOMForbes' Future of Work newsletter features headlines, insights and ideas about where the world of work is headed next. Sign-up instantly here and we'll see you in your inbox on Fridays.Forbes is looking for bold changemakers to highlight as part of ten new Under 30 Local lists in the vibrant innovation hubs of Atlanta, Austin, Boston, Chicago, Cleveland, Los Angeles, Miami, Puerto Rico, Seattle and Toronto. The deadline to nominate someone (or yourself!) is June 9.Thanks for reading! Follow us on Twitter for by-the-minute updates on the latest business and financial news throughout the day. Get unlimited access to premium journalism, exclusive events and more.Limited-Time Only: Save 50% Unsubscribe from Careers.Manage Email Preferences My Forbes Account | Newsletters | Email Help | PrivacyForbes Media 499 Washington Blvd. Jersey City, NJ 07310",1,1

To protect the security of your account, PayPal employs some of the most advanced security systems in the world and our anti-fraud teams regularly screen the PayPal system for unusual activity. Recently, our Account Review Team identified some unusual activity in your account. In accordance with PayPal's User Agreement and to ensure that your account has not been compromised, access to your account was limited. Your account access will remain limited until this issue has been resolved. This is a fraud prevention measure meant to ensure that your account is not compromised. In order to secure your account and quickly restore full access, we may require some specific information from you for the following reason: We would like to ensure that your account was not accessed by an unauthorized third party.
